narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical level for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your Year 1 school decile rank of 8th (stronger than the average of about 6th), a lower Year 3 decile of 3rd (below the typical 6th), upper-middle family income, full-time student status, and notably your LSAT score of 26, which is well below the dataset average of 37. It ultimately predicted a 43% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of admitting students who might struggle post-graduation. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with higher bar exam risks in the data. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your strong Year 1 decile performance, which pulled the prediction in a more favorable direction by showing early academic promise above the norm. Your undergraduate GPA also helped nudge things positively, aligning closely with successful students' averages and signaling consistent college-level ability. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your LSAT stands out as the biggest gap, while your early grades and GPA put you on par or better in those areas. Full-time status, shared by over 90% of students, didn't sway things much either way. This combination led the model to see your overall profile as riskier than those it greenlights. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that's the top lever here—many students improve significantly with targeted prep. You might also highlight any upward trends from Year 1 or seek recommendations emphasizing your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive academics. Exploring part-time programs or bridge courses could provide data points to reassess your bar readiness, opening doors at schools with more flexible criteria. Keep pushing; your strong start shows real potential.
